topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical jabs against my skin. I stood by the velvet rope, my jaw tight enough to crack enamel. There, hanging center stage, was my latest series—the one I’d poured six months of sleepless nights into. A gaping crowd, murmuring appreciatively at the canvases I hadn't even authorized them to be seen. A man in a tailored suit, clearly a patron, leaned in close to one piece, his expression one of genuine awe. I watched him, a cold knot tightening in my stomach. I took a deliberate, slow breath, feeling the heat rise in my neck. This was an insult, a blatant disregard for the boundaries I’d painstakingly established. I wanted to see that look of bewildered disappointment cross his face. Instead, I just stared at the floor, focusing on the pattern of the expensive rug, letting the simmering resentment boil just beneath my composure.

[story 2]

"It's quite captivating, isn't it?" a voice drifted over. I didn't need to turn around to know it was Marcus, the curator, whose smug smile was practically vibrating in the air. I clenched my fists inside my coat pockets, the fabric bunching under the pressure. He had arranged this entire exhibition without consulting me, a unilateral decision that felt like a personal slight. I walked toward a nearby sculpture, my footsteps deliberately loud on the polished floor. I let my gaze sweep over the crowd, making sure my eyes lingered just long enough on the appreciative faces, conveying a silent message: *This is not how I wanted this to go.* A sharp, involuntary twitch pulled at the corner of my mouth, a gesture I immediately smoothed away. I felt a fierce need to disrupt the harmony, to introduce a discordant note into their perfect viewing experience.

[story 3]

The sheer audacity of it made my vision swim slightly. My work, my very voice, was being presented as some sort of public spectacle, curated by someone else. I watched a young woman point excitedly at a piece, her enthusiasm completely unburdened by any sense of ownership or context. A wave of intense irritation washed over me, making my hands tremble slightly. I moved to the edge of the room, positioning myself so that I could observe the interaction, a silent sentinel of displeasure. I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, a defensive posture that radiated a clear message of refusal. When the patron finally moved on, I felt a strange, sharp satisfaction—a momentary triumph born from being the immovable object in their carefully constructed display. It was a small, internal victory, fueled entirely by the feeling of being entirely sidelined.
